name: rpmalloc
summary: Mattias Jansson's lock-free per-thread allocator built on fixed 64 KiB span alignment so the free path can find the span header by masking low address bits, with no central table lookup.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Mattias Jansson's rpmalloc is a lock-free per-thread allocator built on fixed 64 KiB span alignment. The clever trick: aligning every span on 64 KiB means the free path can find the span header by masking low address bits — there is no central table lookup, no `mmap`-based metadata structure. The design is small, public-domain (Unlicense), and ships with a pluggable memory mapper, making it popular in game engines and embedded contexts.

It sits in the same throughput-oriented family as mimalloc and snmalloc, with a different bias: rpmalloc's design priorities are minimal code, maximal portability, and zero allocation latency on the fast path even under pluggable backends.

sections.title: Span-aligned lock-free cache
sections.content:
Each span is exclusively owned by its allocating thread; cross-thread frees go to a per-span deferred free list and are reclaimed when the owning thread next needs a span. A four-level cache hierarchy (active span, partial-free list, free-span list, global list) with adaptive sizing per size-class smooths the allocation rate without inflating the per-thread footprint.

The 64 KiB alignment is the architectural commitment: it bounds the address-space granularity but eliminates an entire class of metadata lookups on the hot path.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Used in Unreal Engine variants, Haiku OS, and various Rust crates via `rpmalloc-rs`. The Unlicense plus pluggable mapper make it a natural fit for embedded and game-engine contexts where licensing and the ability to retarget the page allocator (to a custom HAL, for example) matter more than absolute peak throughput.
